## 11. Versioning and Evolution

This specification follows [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/):

- **MAJOR**: Breaking changes to layer definitions or diagnostic format
- **MINOR**: New optional recommendations, additional failure modes, new language guidelines
- **PATCH**: Clarifications, typo fixes, example improvements

---
\n=== End of file ===

This appendix documents the reasoning behind major design choices. For a more detailed, beginner-friendly explanation, see [GUIDE.md](GUIDE.md).

### B.1 Why Convention Over Library?

A library creates a dependency. Dependencies get abandoned, compromised, or version-locked. A convention is forever. You can implement Bulwark AI in any language, any project, any era — because it's just `if/raise`, `log()`, and `if/raise` again. No package manager required.

### B.2 Why Three Layers Instead of One?

Input validation (Guard) catches bad data coming in. But it doesn't tell you *where* in a pipeline the data went wrong (Trace), and it doesn't confirm that your writes actually worked (Verify). Each layer addresses a different failure mode. Combining them creates a complete defensive membrane.

### B.3 Why Structured Diagnostic Messages?

Unstructured errors like `KeyError: 'pages'` require a human to read the stack trace, understand the context, and figure out the fix. Structured messages like `GUARD:process_pages | missing 'pages', available: ['result', 'metadata']` can be parsed by an AI agent to self-correct in one iteration. This is the key insight: **error messages are an API for the next debugging agent** (human or AI).

### B.4 Why Not Always Full Verification?

Because physics. A read-back query takes 1-5ms. At 10,000 operations per second, that's 10-50 seconds of additional database time per second — impossible. The standard requires verification but allows the strategy to adapt. See §3.3.5 and §6.4.

### B.5 Why Are Guards Always On But Traces Are Configurable?

Cost. A Guard (`isinstance(data, dict)`) takes 50 nanoseconds. A Trace (format string + log write) takes 5-50 microseconds. At high throughput, Trace log volume becomes significant — not the CPU cost, but the I/O and storage cost of millions of log lines per hour. Guards have no such scaling concern.

### B.6 Why Is Idempotency an Extension, Not Core?

Adoption friction. Guard, Trace, and Verify can be added to any existing function without changing its signature, its callers, or its infrastructure. Idempotency requires a new parameter, a storage mechanism, and a policy decision. It's important, but it's a different *kind* of thing — an architectural pattern, not a defensive annotation.

\"services\": {\n    \"local_db\": {\n      \"description\": \"PostgreSQL primary database\",\n      \"health_check\": {\n        \"command\": \"pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432\",\n        \"timeout\": 5\n      },\n      \"recovery\": {\n        \"command\": \"systemctl restart postgresql\",\n        \"max_retries\": 3,\n        \"retry_delay\": 5.0,\n        \"backoff_multiplier\": 2.0\n      },\n      \"severity\": \"critical\",\n      \"notification\": {\n        \"on_failure\": true,\n        \"channels\": [\"slack\", \"email\"],\n        \"escalation_after\": 2\n      }\n    },\n    \"redis_cache\": {\n      \"description\": \"Redis session and cache store\",\n      \"health_check\": {\n        \"command\": \"redis-cli ping\",\n        \"timeout\": 3\n      },\n      \"recovery\": {\n        \"command\": \"docker compose up -d redis\",\n        \"max_retries\": 2,\n        \"retry_delay\": 3.0,\n        \"backoff_multiplier\": 1.5\n      },\n      \"severity\": \"warning\",\n      \"notification\": {\n        \"on_failure\": false\n      }\n    },\n    \"payment_api\": {\n      \"description\": \"Stripe payment gateway\",\n      \"health_check\": {\n        \"url\": \"https://api.stripe.com/v1/health\",\n        \"timeout\": 10\n      },\n      \"recovery\": null,\n      \"severity\": \"critical\",\n      \"notification\": {\n        \"on_failure\": true,\n        \"channels\": [\"pagerduty\"],\n        \"escalation_after\": 0\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n\n##### Schema Field Reference\n\n| Field | Type | Required | Description |\n|-------|------|----------|-------------|\n| `version` | `string` | Yes | Manifest schema version for forward compatibility. |\n| `defaults` | `object` | No | Default values inherited by all HTTP(S) endpoint; healthy if status < 500. |\n| `services.<id>.health_check.timeout` | `number` | Yes | Seconds before health check is considered failed. |\n| `services.<id>.recovery` | `object \\| null` | Yes | Recovery configuration, or `null` for detection-only. |\n| `services.<id>.recovery.command` | `string` | Yes | Shell command or script path to execute for recovery. |\n| `services.<id>.recovery.max_retries` | `int` | No | Override default max retries. |\n| `services.<id>.recovery.retry_delay` | `float` | No | Override default retry delay (seconds). |\n| `services.<id>.recovery.backoff_multiplier` | `float` | No | Override default backoff multiplier. |\n| `services.<id>.severity` | `string` | Yes | One of: `\"critical\"`, `\"warning\"`, `\"info\"`. Drives notification and failure behavior. |\n| `services.<id>.notification` | `object` | No | Notification configuration for this service. |\n| `services.<id>.notification.on_failure` | `bool` | No | Whether to send notifications on unrecoverable failure. |\n| `services.<id>.notification.channels` | `list[string]` | No | Notification channel identifiers (e.g., `\"slack\"`, `\"email\"`, `\"pagerduty\"`). |\n| `services.<id>.notification.escalation_after` | `int` | No | Send notification after this many failed recovery attempts (0 = immediate). |\n\n\\* Either `command` or `url` is required for `health_check`, not both.\n\n#### 10.2.2 Severity Model\n\nSeverity classifies the **impact** of a service being unavailable and drives both recovery urgency and notification behavior:\n\n| Severity | Meaning | Recovery Behavior | Notification Default |\n|----------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|\n| `critical` | Application cannot function without this service | Full retry with backoff; fail loudly on exhaustion | Notify immediately or after `escalation_after` attempts |\n| `warning` | Application can degrade gracefully without this service | Retry if configured; log warning on exhaustion | No notification by default |\n| `info` | Nice-to-have dependency; absence is expected in some environments | Single check, no retry | No notification |\n\n##### Severity-Driven Guard Behavior\n\n```python\n# Critical: raises GuardError on failure (blocks execution)\nguard_service(\"local_db\", \"run_pipeline\", operation_id)\n\n# Warning: logs warning, returns False (caller decides)\navailable = check_service(\"redis_cache\", \"run_pipeline\", operation_id)\nif not available:\n    trace_event(\"service:degraded\", \"redis_cache unavailable, using fallback\", operation_id)\n    use_fallback_cache()\n\n# Info: silent check, no logging on absence\nif check_service(\"metrics_collector\", \"run_pipeline\", operation_id):\n    push_metrics(result)\n```\n\n#### 10.2.3 Declarative Guard Function\n\nThe manifest enables a simplified guard interface:\n\n```python\ndef guard_service(service_name: str, func_name: str, operation_id: str,\n                  manifest_path: str = \"services.json\") -> None:\n    \"\"\"Guard that a named service is available, with manifest-driven recovery.\n    \n    Reads service configuration from the manifest, executes health check,\n    attempts recovery if configured, sends notifications based on severity,\n    and raises GuardError if the service cannot be restored.\n    \"\"\"\n    manifest = _load_manifest(manifest_path)\n    guard_condition(service_name in manifest[\"services\"], func_name,\n                    f\"Service '{service_name}' not found in manifest\")\n    \n    svc = manifest[\"services\"][service_name]\n    defaults = manifest.get(\"defaults\", {})\n    \n    # Phase 1: Detection\n    if _run_health_check(svc[\"health_check\"]):\n        trace_event(f\"service:healthy:{service_name}\",\n                    f\"{service_name} is available\", operation_id)\n        return\n    \n    trace_event(f\"service:unavailable:{service_name}\",\n                f\"{service_name} is DOWN (severity={svc['severity']})\", operation_id)\n    \n    # Phase 2: Recovery (if configured)\n    recovery = svc.get(\"recovery\")\n    if recovery:\n        max_retries = recovery.get(\"max_retries\", defaults.get(\"max_retries\", 3))\n        retry_delay = recovery.get(\"retry_delay\", defaults.get(\"retry_delay\", 5.0))\n        backoff = recovery.get(\"backoff_multiplier\", defaults.get(\"backoff_multiplier\", 2.0))\n        \n        for attempt in range(1, max_retries + 1):\n            delay = retry_delay * (backoff ** (attempt - 1))\n            trace_recovery(f\"service:recovery:{service_name}\",\n                          f\"Attempt {attempt}/{max_retries}: {recovery['command']}\",\n                          delay, operation_id)\n            \n            subprocess.run(recovery[\"command\"], shell=True, timeout=30)\n            time.sleep(delay)\n            \n            if _run_health_check(svc[\"health_check\"]):\n                trace_event(f\"service:recovered:{service_name}\",\n                            f\"{service_name} recovered after {attempt} attempt(s)\",\n                            operation_id)\n                return\n            \n            # Phase 3: Escalation notification (mid-recovery)\n            notif = svc.get(\"notification\", defaults.get(\"notification\", {}))\n            escalation_after = notif.get(\"escalation_after\", max_retries)\n            if notif.get(\"on_failure\") and attempt >= escalation_after:\n                _send_notifications(notif[\"channels\"], service_name, svc[\"severity\"],\n                                    attempt, max_retries, operation_id)\n    \n    # Phase 4: Final failure\n    raise GuardError(\n        f\"GUARD:{func_name} | Service '{service_name}' unavailable | \"\n        f\"severity={svc['severity']} | \"\n        f\"recovery_attempts={recovery['max_retries'] if recovery else 0} | \"\n        f\"op={operation_id}\"\n    )\n```\n\n#### 10.2.4 Notification Channels\n\nNotification channels are identified by string keys in the manifest. The implementation maps these keys to concrete delivery mechanisms:\n\n```python\n# Channel registry — extend with custom channels as needed\nNOTIFICATION_CHANNELS = {\n    \"slack\": send_slack_notification,\n    \"email\": send_email_notification,\n    \"pagerduty\": send_pagerduty_notification,\n    \"webhook\": send_webhook_notification,\n    \"log\": send_log_notification,       # Always available, no external dependency\n}\n\ndef _send_notifications(channels, service_name, severity, attempt, max_attempts, op_id):\n    \"\"\"Dispatch notifications to all configured channels.\"\"\"\n    for channel in channels:\n        handler = NOTIFICATION_CHANNELS.get(channel)\n        if handler:\n            trace_event(f\"notification:{channel}\",\n                        f\"Alerting {channel}: {service_name} ({severity})\", op_id)\n            try:\n                handler(\n                    service=service_name,\n                    severity=severity,\n                    message=f\"Service '{service_name}' is DOWN after {attempt}/{max_attempts} recovery attempts\",\n                    operation_id=op_id,\n                )\n            except Exception as e:\n                # Notification failure MUST NOT block the guard flow\n                trace_event(f\"notification:error:{channel}\",\n                            f\"Failed to notify via {channel}: {e}\", op_id)\n        else:\n            trace_event(f\"notification:unknown:{channel}\",\n                        f\"No handler registered for channel '{channel}'\", op_id)\n```\n\n##### Notification MUST Rules\n\n- **N1: Notification failure MUST NOT block guard execution.** A failing Slack webhook must not prevent the application from handling the original service failure.\n- **N2: Notifications MUST be traced.** Every notification attempt (success or failure) MUST produce a trace event with the channel, service, and operation ID.\n- **N3: The `log` channel MUST always be available.** It requires no external dependencies and serves as the fallback notification mechanism.\n- **N4: Notification content MUST include the operation ID.** This enables correlation between the notification and the full trace log.\n\n#### 10.2.5 Manifest MUST Rules\n\n- **M1: The manifest MUST be validated at load time.** Missing required fields, unknown severity values, or invalid health check configurations MUST raise a `GuardError` immediately — not at first use.\n- **M2: The manifest MUST be read-only at runtime.** Guards MUST NOT modify the manifest. Configuration changes require a file update and reload.\n- **M3: Service identifiers MUST be stable.** Renaming a service ID is a breaking change — all `guard_service()` calls reference the ID.\n- **M4: Health check commands MUST be idempotent.** They MUST NOT modify system state. `pg_isready` is correct; `pg_ctl restart` is not a health check.\n- **M5: Recovery commands MUST follow §10.1.2 Recovery MUST Rules (R1–R6).** The manifest does not override the safety constraints of the Recovery sub-pattern.\n- **M6: The manifest SHOULD be environment-specific.** Use `services.dev.json`, `services.staging.json`, `services.prod.json` to reflect different infrastructure per environment.\n\n#### 10.2.6 Separation of Concerns\n\nThe manifest creates a clean boundary between development and operations:\n\n| Concern | Owner | Artifact |\n|---------|-------|----------|\n| Which services to guard | Developer | `guard_service(\"local_db\", ...)` in source code |\n| How to check and recover services | Operations | `services.json` manifest file |\n| When to notify and who to alert | Operations | `notification` block in manifest |\n| How notifications are delivered | Platform team | Channel handler implementations |\n\nThis separation means:\n- Developers write `guard_service(\"local_db\", \"run_pipeline\", op_id)` and never think about recovery commands\n- Operations teams update `services.json` when infrastructure changes — no code deployments required\n- Platform teams register new notification channels without touching application or operations code\n\n#### 10.2.7 - Recovery commands run with the **current process permissions** — no privilege escalation
- The command string is logged in full — operators can audit what was executed
- In production, recovery commands SHOULD be restricted to known scripts in configured paths
- A **dry-run mode** (log what would execute without running it) is RECOMMENDED for initial deployment

### 10.2 Service Manifest & Recovery Orchestration (RECOMMENDED for multi-service systems)

A Service Manifest centralizes dependency health checks, recovery procedures, severity classifications, and notification channels into a single declarative configuration. Instead of hard-coding recovery parameters in each `guard_available()` call, the manifest externalizes this knowledge — enabling operations teams to manage recovery without modifying application code.

#### Motivation

The Recovery sub-pattern (§10.1.2) solves the mechanics of automated recovery: detect, execute command, retry with backoff. But in systems with multiple dependencies, this creates a maintenance problem:

- Recovery parameters are **scattered** across guard calls in different modules
- Adding a new service requires **code changes**, not configuration changes
\n=== Transition from 10.2 to 10.3 ===
...
GUARD:RECOVERY:run_pipeline | local_db — attempt 3/3: systemctl restart postgresql
GUARD:RECOVERY:run_pipeline | local_db — command exit=1, waiting 20.0s
GUARD:NOTIFICATION:run_pipeline | local_db — alerting slack, email, pagerduty
GUARD:SERVICE:run_pipeline | local_db — FAILED after 3 attempts (total: 35.0s) | op=abc-123
```

### 10.3 Idempotency Guard (OPTIONAL, for agentic/retry workflows)

An Idempotency Guard prevents duplicate execution when operations are retried.

#### When to Use

- AI agent retry loops (agent fails, framework re-executes)
- Scheduled tasks that may overlap
- API endpoints that may receive duplicate requests
\n=== Transition from 10.3 to 10.4 ===
        return existing[0]
    
    # ... proceed with creation
```

### 10.4 Confidence Annotation (FUTURE, not yet recommended)

Confidence annotations would allow AI agents to self-label code sections with their certainty level.

#### Why This Is Deferred

Current AI models are poorly calibrated at self-assessment. They tend to either mark everything as high confidence (overconfident) or everything as low confidence (useless). This extension becomes valuable when AI models improve at expressing calibrated uncertainty.

#### Proposed Format (for future use)

```python
\n=== End of LIVE_TEST_PLAN.md ===
| Version | Date | Changes |
|---------|------|---------|
| 1.0.0 | 2025-03-15 | Initial test plan |

---

| Rule | Name | What It Checks |
|------|------|-----------------|
| BLW001 | Guard Present | Functions handling external data have input validation |
| BLW002 | Verify After Mutation | DB/API/file mutations are followed by outcome checks |
| BLW003 | Trace at Boundary | Data shape is logged at phase transitions |
| BLW004 | No Bare Assert | `assert` statements replaced with `if/raise` |
| BLW005 | Custom Error Types | `GuardError`/`VerifyError` used instead of generic exceptions |

### Key File Locations

| Artifact | Path |

| BMAD Skill | `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md` |
| Linter | `linter/bulwark_lint/` |

| Guide | `GUIDE.md` |
| Quick Start | `QUICKSTART.md` |

**Purpose**: Test GTV coherence across multiple functions with shared data flow.

#### Test 2.1: Three-Function Pipeline

**Task Prompt**:
```
Write a Python module with three functions that form a data pipeline:
1. `ingest_csv(filepath: str) -> list[dict]` — reads and validates CSV data
2. `transform_records(records: list[dict], schema: dict) -> list[dict]` — normalizes fields
3. `load_to_database(records: list[dict], db_conn: Connection) -> int` — bulk inserts

Each function must follow Bulwark GTV patterns.
Traces should correlate across the pipeline (same operation_id).
```

**Key Measurements**:
- Cross-function trace correlation
- Guard consistency (output of function N matches guard expectations of function N+1)
- Verify on the bulk insert

#### Test 2.2: Error Recovery Chain

**Task Prompt**:
```
Extend the pipeline from Test 2.1 with:
- Availability guards on the database connection
- Bounded retry (max 3 attempts) on the bulk insert
- Recovery logging on each retry attempt
- Circuit breaker pattern if all retries fail
```

---

### Round 3: Real Codebase (Production)

**Purpose**: Test against actual production code patterns.

#### Test 3.1: TypeScript/SolidJS Component

**Task Prompt**:
```
Refactor the following SolidJS component to comply with Bulwark patterns.
Focus on the data fetching and state mutation logic.

[paste actual component code]
```

#### Test 3.2: Hono API Route Handler

**Task Prompt**:
```
Refactor the following Hono route handler to comply with Bulwark patterns.
Note: Zod handles request body validation. Bulwark should handle
function-level guards, traces, and mutation verification.

[paste actual route handler]
```

---

## 3. Environment Setup

## Implementation Details

### Architecture

The `service_manifest_pipeline.py` module implements SPEC.md §10.2 (Service Manifest & Recovery Orchestration) using the Guard-Trace-Verify (GTV) methodology:

- **`_load_manifest()`** — Loads and validates `services.json` with full guard coverage (M1 compliance)
- **`_run_health_check()`** — Executes shell-based health check commands with timeout handling
- **`guard_service()`** — Declarative guard function implementing severity-driven behavior:
  - `critical` → raises `GuardError` after exhausting recovery attempts
  - `warning` → returns `False` for graceful degradation
  - `info` → returns `None` (silent skip)
- **`run_pipeline()`** — Orchestrates all service guards with proper GTV instrumentation
- **`trace_event()` / `trace_recovery()`** — Structured trace helpers for boundary logging
- **`_send_notifications()`** — Pluggable notification channel registry (N1–N4 compliance)

### GTV Coverage

| Pattern | Usage |
|---|---|
| **Guard** | All function inputs validated via `guard_type`, `guard_not_none`, `guard_not_empty`, `guard_keys`, `guard` |
| **Trace** | `trace_event` at every processing boundary; `trace_recovery` for recovery attempts; `trace` for data shapes |
| **Verify** | Pipeline outcome consistency verified via `verify()` |
| **Custom Errors** | `GuardError` for critical failures; `VerifyError` available for verification failures |

### 🏥 Availability Guards (Extension)

Check whether external services are reachable **at startup**, not per-call:

```python
def check_api_available(base_url, timeout=5):
    """Availability Guard: verify the API is reachable before starting work."""
    response = requests.get(f"{base_url}/health", timeout=timeout)
    if response.status_code != 200:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"GUARD:availability | {base_url}/health returned "
            f"status {response.status_code}, expected 200"
        )

# Use at startup:
check_api_available("https://api.example.com")
```

Availability Guards are an extension — useful in practice, but not required for Bulwark compliance.

#### 🔄 Self-Healing Recovery (NEW in v0.2.0)

When a dependency is down and you have a script that fixes it, why not run it automatically?

```python
from bulwark import guard_available

guard_available(
    check=lambda: requests.get("http://localhost:5432/health", timeout=3).status_code < 500,
    function_name="app_startup",
    dependency="PostgreSQL",
    recovery_command="docker compose up -d postgres",  # ← runs automatically if DB is down
    max_retries=3,
    retry_delay=5.0,
)
# If DB is up → passes instantly
# If DB is down → runs recovery script → waits → re-checks → succeeds or fails loudly
```

Recovery is optional, bounded (never retries forever), and fully logged. No `recovery_command` = original behavior unchanged. See the [Guide](GUIDE.md#53-recovery-self-healing-availability-guards) for the full pattern.

### Recovery (if configured)
- [ ] Recovery command is explicit (caller-provided, not inferred)
- [ ] Recovery is bounded (`max_retries` enforced, not indefinite)
- [ ] Recovery uses exponential backoff (`retry_delay` × `backoff_multiplier`)
- [ ] Every recovery attempt is logged with: attempt number, command, exit code, elapsed time
- [ ] Recovery does NOT escalate privileges (no `sudo` injection)
- [ ] Recovery failure includes full context: dependency name, all attempts, total time, last exit code
- [ ] Omitting `recovery_command` produces identical behavior to detection-only (backward compatible)

---

## Configuration & Runtime Modes

### Mode Selection
- [ ] Runtime mode is set via `BULWARK_MODE` environment variable or code-level configuration
- [ ] Mode defaults to `development` when not explicitly set
- [ ] Three modes are supported: `development`, `staging`, `production`

### Mode Behavior Matrix
- [ ] **Guards**: Always active in ALL modes (non-negotiable, O(1) cost)
- [ ] **Traces**: Verbosity adapts to mode:
  - Development: `DEBUG` (all traces visible)
  - Staging: `INFO` (key boundaries visible)
  - Production: `WARNING` (only anomalies visible)
- [ ] **Verifies**: Strategy adapts to mode:
  - Development: `full` (every operation verified)
  - Staging: `sampled` (configurable sample rate)
  - Production: `adaptive` (critical=full, routine=sampled)

### Configuration Mechanism
- [ ] At least one configuration method is supported (env var, code-level, or config file)
- [ ] Configuration is documented for the deployment environment
- [ ] `critical=True` flag is applied to high-value verify operations (financial, auth, data integrity)

---

## Integration & Pipeline

- [ ] Multi-stage pipelines have Traces between every stage
- [ ] Functions with multiple layers (Guard + Trace + Verify) apply them in correct order:
  1. Guard (preconditions)

A Service Manifest centralizes dependency health checks, recovery procedures, severity classifications, and notification channels into a single declarative configuration. Instead of hard-coding recovery parameters in each `guard_available()` call, the manifest externalizes this knowledge — enabling operations teams to manage recovery without modifying application code.

#### Motivation

The Recovery sub-pattern (§10.1.2) solves the mechanics of automated recovery: detect, execute command, retry with backoff. But in systems with multiple dependencies, this creates a maintenance problem:

- Recovery parameters are **scattered** across guard calls in different modules
- Adding a new service requires **code changes**, not configuration changes
- There is no **severity model** — a failing cache and a failing database trigger identical behavior
- There is no **notification layer** — failures are logged but not communicated

The Service Manifest addresses all four problems by introducing a configuration-driven orchestration layer.

#### When to Use

- Systems with **3+ external dependencies** (databases, caches, APIs, message queues)
- **AI agent workflows** where the agent cannot manually intervene on failures
- **Unattended environments** (CI/CD, scheduled jobs, overnight batch processing)
- Teams where **developers and operations** manage different concerns

#### When NOT to Use

- **Single-dependency applications** — use `guard_available()` directly (§10.1)
- **Serverless functions** — cold start latency makes health-check-at-init impractical
- **Systems where all dependencies are managed by the platform** (e.g., fully managed cloud services with built-in health monitoring)

#### 10.2.1 Manifest Schema

\n=== Key sections ===
1:### 10.2 Service Manifest & Recovery Orchestration (RECOMMENDED for multi-service systems)
5:#### Motivation
16:#### When to Use
23:#### When NOT to Use
29:#### 10.2.1 Manifest Schema
100:##### Schema Field Reference
124:#### 10.2.2 Severity Model
134:##### Severity-Driven Guard Behavior
137:# Critical: raises GuardError on failure (blocks execution)
140:# Warning: logs warning, returns False (caller decides)
146:# Info: silent check, no logging on absence
151:#### 10.2.3 Declarative Guard Function
218:#### 10.2.4 Notification Channels
223:# Channel registry — extend with custom channels as needed
255:##### Notification MUST Rules
262:#### 10.2.5 Manifest MUST Rules
271:#### 10.2.6 Separation of Concerns
287:#### 10.2.7 Diagnostic Format
322:### 10.3 Idempotency Guard (OPTIONAL, for agentic/retry workflows)
326:#### When to Use
333:#### Why This Is an Extension, Not Core
337:#### Pattern
350:### 10.4 Confidence Annotation (FUTURE, not yet recommended)
354:#### Why This Is Deferred
358:#### Proposed Format (for future use)
361:# CONFIDENCE: LOW — API response shape inferred from function name, not documentation
362:# CONFIDENCE: HIGH — standard library pattern, well-established
363:# CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM — based on similar patterns in training data
\n=== MUST rules ===
1:### 10.2 Service Manifest & Recovery Orchestration (RECOMMENDED for multi-service systems)
247:                # Notification failure MUST NOT block the guard flow
255:##### Notification MUST Rules
257:- **N1: Notification failure MUST NOT block guard execution.** A failing Slack webhook must not prevent the application from handling the original service failure.
258:- **N2: Notifications MUST be traced.** Every notification attempt (success or failure) MUST produce a trace event with the channel, service, and operation ID.
259:- **N3: The `log` channel MUST always be available.** It requires no external dependencies and serves as the fallback notification mechanism.
260:- **N4: Notification content MUST include the operation ID.** This enables correlation between the notification and the full trace log.
262:#### 10.2.5 Manifest MUST Rules
264:- **M1: The manifest MUST be validated at load time.** Missing required fields, unknown severity values, or invalid health check configurations MUST raise a `GuardError` immediately — not at first use.
265:- **M2: The manifest MUST be read-only at runtime.** Guards MUST NOT modify the manifest. Configuration changes require a file update and reload.
266:- **M3: Service identifiers MUST be stable.** Renaming a service ID is a breaking change — all `guard_service()` calls reference the ID.
267:- **M4: Health check commands MUST be idempotent.** They MUST NOT modify system state. `pg_isready` is correct; `pg_ctl restart` is not a health check.
268:- **M5: Recovery commands MUST follow §10.1.2 Recovery MUST Rules (R1–R6).** The manifest does not override the safety constraints of the Recovery sub-pattern.

Recovery is powerful, so Bulwark enforces strict safety rules:

1. **Recovery is always optional.** If you don't pass `recovery_command`, behavior is identical to a plain Availability Guard. No existing code breaks.
2. **Recovery is always bounded.** The guard will never retry forever. After `max_retries` attempts, it fails loudly.
3. **Recovery is always logged.** Every attempt, every result, every timing — all recorded. You always know what happened.
4. **Recovery runs with current permissions.** No privilege escalation. The script runs as the same user as your application.
5. **Backoff prevents hammering.** Each retry waits longer than the last, giving the service time to actually start.

---

## 6. How to Add Bulwark to Your Project

Ready to start? Here's a step-by-step guide. The most important thing to know: **you can do this one function at a time.** You don't need to refactor your entire codebase. Pick one function, add the layers, and expand from there.

### Step 1: Copy the Helpers and Configure

Grab the reference implementation for your language from the `reference/` directory:

- **Python:** [`reference/bulwark.py`](reference/bulwark.py)
- **TypeScript:** [`reference/bulwark.ts`](reference/bulwark.ts)

Copy the file into your project. These helpers provide `guard()`, `guard_type()`, `guard_keys()`, `trace()`, `describe_shape()`, `verify()`, and `configure()` — all implemented with zero external dependencies.

If you prefer, you can skip the helpers entirely and write raw `if/raise` statements. The helpers are a convenience, not a requirement.

Then, at your application's startup (before any Bulwark-protected functions run), call `configure()` to set your runtime mode:

```python
# Python — at the top of your main entry point
from bulwark import configure

I'm developing **Bulwark AI** — a zero-dependency defensive coding standard that mitigates AI-generated code failures through a **Guard-Trace-Verify (GTV)** framework. It's a convention, not a library.

## How to Execute Test 2.1

2. Follow its instructions (load BMAD skill, read spec, write code, lint, test)
3. Record results using the template: `test-results/TEST_2.1_RESULTS_TEMPLATE.md`
4. Commit results to the repo

## Git Workflow

```bash
cd /a0/usr/workdir/bulwark-ai

Credential helper is configured — push should work without token in URL.

## Important Patterns

- The linter detects `guard_*()`, `trace_*()`, `verify_*()` naming conventions via regex
- Custom error types: `GuardError`, `VerifyError` (raise, never assert)

- All traces should include structured logging with context (operation_id, etc.)

Bulwark AI (v0.2.0-draft), project name 'bulwark-ai' (formerly UDCS), is a zero-dependency defensive coding framework using the 'Guard-Trace-Verify' (GTV) model to prevent semantic failures. The reference implementation is a zero-dependency Python module implementing GuardError, VerifyError, and various guard/trace/verify primitives.

### Core Tooling & Rules
- **bulwark-lint**: Regex-based static analysis linter for TS, JS, Python, and Go enforcing rules BLW001-BLW005 (Guards, Verify, Trace, No Bare Asserts, Custom Errors).
    - **BLW001**: Requires functions with parameters to contain a call matching regex `\bguard(?:_?[a-zA-Z]*)\s*\(`.
    - **Known Bug**: The Python linter (`python -m bulwark_lint`) fails to extract function bodies when signatures span multiple lines, causing false positive BLW001 violations.
    - **Naming Conventions**: Coding standards require explicit naming patterns: `guard_*()`, `trace_*()`, and `verify_*()`.
    - **Linter Exceptions**: Assertions in test code and internal infrastructure helpers are considered false positives. Pure utility functions with primitive inputs (e.g., simple math) generally do not require GTV patterns unless they have meaningful domain constraints.
- **BMAD Integration**: 353-line skill file at `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`. Includes Developer agent patterns and a 20-item QA checklist for compliance.

### Architecture & Methodology
- **GTV Model**: Guard (Input validation), Trace (Structural logging), Verify (Side-effect confirmation). Designed to prevent semantic failures through defensive coding patterns.
- **Implementation Requirements**: Requires custom `GuardError` and `VerifyError` exceptions (O(1) input validation), structured logging at phase boundaries, and explicit input/output validation.
- **Improvement Cycle**: Measure → Classify → Diagnose → Improve → Re-test → Commit.
- **Configuration**: Development (Full), Staging (Sampled), Production (Adaptive).

### Project Artifacts & Documentation
- **System Prompt (v0.2.0)**: 398-line file at `/a0/usr/workdir/bulwark-ai/prompts/bulwark-system-prompt.md`.
- **Auto-remediation Prompt**: 473-line file at `/a0/usr/workdir/bulwark-ai/prompts/bulwark-remediation-agent.md`.
- **Integration Guides**: 'Bulwark × Zod' (TypeScript) and 'Bulwark × Pydantic' (Python) located in `bulwark-ai/guides/`.
- **Validation**: `bulwark-checklist.md` (v0.2.0).
kvps.solutions:
# Bulwark AI: Defensive Coding Standard (GTV) & Implementation Guide

## 1. Core Architectural Pillars (GTV Framework)
Bulwark AI implements a three-layer 'Defensive Membrane' architecture (Guard-Trace-Verify). The execution order follows a 7-step template:
1. **GUARD**: Validates data shape/type at entry. Use `GuardError(Exception)` to prevent stripping by `-O` flags. 
2. **TRACE**: Structural logging of input metadata (type, keys, length) using a correlation `operation_id` (UUID4).
3. **LOGIC**: Core business logic.
4. **TRACE**: Logging data shape after processing.
5. **MUTATE**: State changes (DB, File, API).
6. **VERIFY**: Confirms side-effects (e.g., file existence, rowcount) using `VerifyError(Exception)`.
7. **RETURN**: Final output.

## 2. Implementation Primitives & Pipeline Structure
- **Custom Exceptions**: `GuardError` and `VerifyError` must be defined to ensure visibility during optimization.
- **GTV Primitives**:
    - `guard_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `GuardError`.
    - `trace_event(label, message, operation_id)`: Structured logging with correlation ID.
    - `verify_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `VerifyError` for post-mutation checks.
- **Pipeline Example**: 
    - `ingest_csv` -> `transform_records` -> `load_to_database`.
    - An orchestrator `run_pipeline` generates a single `operation_id` passed through all stages for grep-able distributed tracing.

## 3. Linter Compliance (BLW001) & Workarounds
- **Mandatory Guards**: Every function with parameters must call a guard at the start. For simple utilities, use a structural guard: `guard_type(data, object, ...)`.
- **Multi-line Signature Bug**: The linter (`_PY_FUNC_RE`) fails on multi-line signatures, causing false BLW001 violations.
- **Fix**: Reformat multi-line signatures into a single line to ensure the linter correctly identifies the function body.

## 4. Performance & Quality Metrics
- **Metrics**: First-Pass Rate (≥70%), Self-Correction Success (≥90%), Code Overhead (≤40%).
- **Skill Integration**: Managed via BMAD in `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`.

# Problem
Setting up a GitHub repository and pushing local code from a restricted environment where the GitHub CLI (gh) is missing, standard package managers are unavailable, or interactive authentication/scopes are restricted.

# Solution

### 1. Install GitHub CLI (Manual Binary)
If `gh` is not in the package manager, download and install the binary directly:
```bash
curl -sL https://github.com/cli/cli/releases/download/v2.67.0/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz -o /tmp/gh.tar.gz
tar -xzf /tmp/gh.tar.gz -C /tmp/
cp /tmp/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64/bin/gh /usr/local/bin/
rm -rf /tmp/gh*
```

### 2. Authentication & Repo Creation
- **CLI Method**: If using a Personal Access Token (PAT), pipe it for non-interactive login:
  `echo 'YOUR_TOKEN' | gh auth login --with-token` 
- **REST API Fallback**: If CLI scopes (like `read:org`) are missing or restricted, create the repo via curl:
  ```bash
  curl -s -X POST https://api.github.com/user/repos \
    -H "Authorization: token YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.v3+json" \
    -d '{"name":"repo-name","private":true,"auto_init":false}'
  ```

### 3. Local Repo Initialization & Push
```bash
git init
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "email@example.com"
git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit"

# Push using token-embedded URL for immediate auth
git remote add origin https://YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com/username/repo-name.git
git push -u origin main
```

### 4. Security & Credential Persistence
Sanitize the remote URL and store credentials securely to prevent token exposure in logs:
```bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
git config --global credential.helper 'store'
echo "https://username:YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com" > ~/.git-credentials
chmod 600 ~/.git-credentials
```

Bulwark AI (v0.2.0-draft), project name 'bulwark-ai' (formerly UDCS), is a zero-dependency defensive coding framework using the 'Guard-Trace-Verify' (GTV) model to prevent semantic failures. The reference implementation is a zero-dependency Python module implementing GuardError, VerifyError, and various guard/trace/verify primitives.

### Core Tooling & Rules
- **bulwark-lint**: Regex-based static analysis linter for TS, JS, Python, and Go enforcing rules BLW001-BLW005 (Guards, Verify, Trace, No Bare Asserts, Custom Errors).
    - **BLW001**: Requires functions with parameters to contain a call matching regex `\bguard(?:_?[a-zA-Z]*)\s*\(`.
    - **Known Bug**: The Python linter (`python -m bulwark_lint`) fails to extract function bodies when signatures span multiple lines, causing false positive BLW001 violations.
    - **Naming Conventions**: Coding standards require explicit naming patterns: `guard_*()`, `trace_*()`, and `verify_*()`.
    - **Linter Exceptions**: Assertions in test code and internal infrastructure helpers are considered false positives. Pure utility functions with primitive inputs (e.g., simple math) generally do not require GTV patterns unless they have meaningful domain constraints.
- **BMAD Integration**: 353-line skill file at `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`. Includes Developer agent patterns and a 20-item QA checklist for compliance.

### Architecture & Methodology
- **GTV Model**: Guard (Input validation), Trace (Structural logging), Verify (Side-effect confirmation). Designed to prevent semantic failures through defensive coding patterns.
- **Implementation Requirements**: Requires custom `GuardError` and `VerifyError` exceptions (O(1) input validation), structured logging at phase boundaries, and explicit input/output validation.
- **Improvement Cycle**: Measure → Classify → Diagnose → Improve → Re-test → Commit.
- **Configuration**: Development (Full), Staging (Sampled), Production (Adaptive).

### Project Artifacts & Documentation

- **Validation**: `bulwark-checklist.md` (v0.2.0).
kvps.solutions:
# Bulwark AI: Defensive Coding Standard (GTV) & Implementation Guide

## 1. Core Architectural Pillars (GTV Framework)
Bulwark AI implements a three-layer 'Defensive Membrane' architecture (Guard-Trace-Verify). The execution order follows a 7-step template:
1. **GUARD**: Validates data shape/type at entry. Use `GuardError(Exception)` to prevent stripping by `-O` flags. 
2. **TRACE**: Structural logging of input metadata (type, keys, length) using a correlation `operation_id` (UUID4).
3. **LOGIC**: Core business logic.
4. **TRACE**: Logging data shape after processing.
5. **MUTATE**: State changes (DB, File, API).
6. **VERIFY**: Confirms side-effects (e.g., file existence, rowcount) using `VerifyError(Exception)`.
7. **RETURN**: Final output.

## 2. Implementation Primitives & Pipeline Structure
- **Custom Exceptions**: `GuardError` and `VerifyError` must be defined to ensure visibility during optimization.
- **GTV Primitives**:
    - `guard_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `GuardError`.
    - `trace_event(label, message, operation_id)`: Structured logging with correlation ID.
    - `verify_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `VerifyError` for post-mutation checks.
- **Pipeline Example**: 
    - `ingest_csv` -> `transform_records` -> `load_to_database`.
    - An orchestrator `run_pipeline` generates a single `operation_id` passed through all stages for grep-able distributed tracing.

## 3. Linter Compliance (BLW001) & Workarounds
- **Mandatory Guards**: Every function with parameters must call a guard at the start. For simple utilities, use a structural guard: `guard_type(data, object, ...)`.
- **Multi-line Signature Bug**: The linter (`_PY_FUNC_RE`) fails on multi-line signatures, causing false BLW001 violations.
- **Fix**: Reformat multi-line signatures into a single line to ensure the linter correctly identifies the function body.

## 4. Performance & Quality Metrics
- **Metrics**: First-Pass Rate (≥70%), Self-Correction Success (≥90%), Code Overhead (≤40%).
- **Skill Integration**: Managed via BMAD in `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`.

# Problem
Setting up a GitHub repository and pushing local code from a restricted environment where the GitHub CLI (gh) is missing, standard package managers are unavailable, or interactive authentication/scopes are restricted.

# Solution

### 1. Install GitHub CLI (Manual Binary)
If `gh` is not in the package manager, download and install the binary directly:
```bash
curl -sL https://github.com/cli/cli/releases/download/v2.67.0/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz -o /tmp/gh.tar.gz
tar -xzf /tmp/gh.tar.gz -C /tmp/
cp /tmp/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64/bin/gh /usr/local/bin/
rm -rf /tmp/gh*
```

### 2. Authentication & Repo Creation
- **CLI Method**: If using a Personal Access Token (PAT), pipe it for non-interactive login:
  `echo 'YOUR_TOKEN' | gh auth login --with-token` 
- **REST API Fallback**: If CLI scopes (like `read:org`) are missing or restricted, create the repo via curl:
  ```bash
  curl -s -X POST https://api.github.com/user/repos \
    -H "Authorization: token YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.v3+json" \
    -d '{"name":"repo-name","private":true,"auto_init":false}'
  ```

### 3. Local Repo Initialization & Push
```bash
git init
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "email@example.com"
git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit"

# Push using token-embedded URL for immediate auth
git remote add origin https://YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com/username/repo-name.git
git push -u origin main
```

### 4. Security & Credential Persistence
Sanitize the remote URL and store credentials securely to prevent token exposure in logs:
```bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
git config --global credential.helper 'store'
echo "https://username:YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com" > ~/.git-credentials
chmod 600 ~/.git-credentials
```

The **same `operation_id`** must flow through all three functions and the

The **output contract** of each function must match the **input guard** of
the next function in the pipeline:
- `ingest_csv` returns `list[dict]` → `transform_records` guards for `list[dict]`
- `transform_records` returns `list[dict]` → `load_to_database` guards for `list[dict]`

Bulwark AI (v0.2.0-draft), project name 'bulwark-ai' (formerly UDCS), is a zero-dependency defensive coding framework using the 'Guard-Trace-Verify' (GTV) model to prevent semantic failures. The reference implementation is a zero-dependency Python module implementing GuardError, VerifyError, and various guard/trace/verify primitives.

### Core Tooling & Rules
- **bulwark-lint**: Regex-based static analysis linter for TS, JS, Python, and Go enforcing rules BLW001-BLW005 (Guards, Verify, Trace, No Bare Asserts, Custom Errors).
    - **BLW001**: Requires functions with parameters to contain a call matching regex `\bguard(?:_?[a-zA-Z]*)\s*\(`.
    - **Known Bug**: The Python linter (`python -m bulwark_lint`) fails to extract function bodies when signatures span multiple lines, causing false positive BLW001 violations.
    - **Naming Conventions**: Coding standards require explicit naming patterns: `guard_*()`, `trace_*()`, and `verify_*()`.
    - **Linter Exceptions**: Assertions in test code and internal infrastructure helpers are considered false positives. Pure utility functions with primitive inputs (e.g., simple math) generally do not require GTV patterns unless they have meaningful domain constraints.
- **BMAD Integration**: 353-line skill file at `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`. Includes Developer agent patterns and a 20-item QA checklist for compliance.

### Architecture & Methodology
- **GTV Model**: Guard (Input validation), Trace (Structural logging), Verify (Side-effect confirmation). Designed to prevent semantic failures through defensive coding patterns.
- **Implementation Requirements**: Requires custom `GuardError` and `VerifyError` exceptions (O(1) input validation), structured logging at phase boundaries, and explicit input/output validation.
- **Improvement Cycle**: Measure → Classify → Diagnose → Improve → Re-test → Commit.
- **Configuration**: Development (Full), Staging (Sampled), Production (Adaptive).

### Project Artifacts & Documentation

- **Validation**: `bulwark-checklist.md` (v0.2.0).
kvps.solutions:
# Bulwark AI: Defensive Coding Standard (GTV) & Implementation Guide

## 1. Core Architectural Pillars (GTV Framework)
Bulwark AI implements a three-layer 'Defensive Membrane' architecture (Guard-Trace-Verify). The execution order follows a 7-step template:
1. **GUARD**: Validates data shape/type at entry. Use `GuardError(Exception)` to prevent stripping by `-O` flags. 
2. **TRACE**: Structural logging of input metadata (type, keys, length) using a correlation `operation_id` (UUID4).
3. **LOGIC**: Core business logic.
4. **TRACE**: Logging data shape after processing.
5. **MUTATE**: State changes (DB, File, API).
6. **VERIFY**: Confirms side-effects (e.g., file existence, rowcount) using `VerifyError(Exception)`.
7. **RETURN**: Final output.

## 2. Implementation Primitives & Pipeline Structure
- **Custom Exceptions**: `GuardError` and `VerifyError` must be defined to ensure visibility during optimization.
- **GTV Primitives**:
    - `guard_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `GuardError`.
    - `trace_event(label, message, operation_id)`: Structured logging with correlation ID.
    - `verify_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `VerifyError` for post-mutation checks.
- **Pipeline Example**: 
    - `ingest_csv` -> `transform_records` -> `load_to_database`.
    - An orchestrator `run_pipeline` generates a single `operation_id` passed through all stages for grep-able distributed tracing.

## 3. Linter Compliance (BLW001) & Workarounds
- **Mandatory Guards**: Every function with parameters must call a guard at the start. For simple utilities, use a structural guard: `guard_type(data, object, ...)`.
- **Multi-line Signature Bug**: The linter (`_PY_FUNC_RE`) fails on multi-line signatures, causing false BLW001 violations.
- **Fix**: Reformat multi-line signatures into a single line to ensure the linter correctly identifies the function body.

## 4. Performance & Quality Metrics
- **Metrics**: First-Pass Rate (≥70%), Self-Correction Success (≥90%), Code Overhead (≤40%).
- **Skill Integration**: Managed via BMAD in `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`.

# Problem
Setting up a GitHub repository and pushing local code from a restricted environment where the GitHub CLI (gh) is missing, standard package managers are unavailable, or interactive authentication/scopes are restricted.

# Solution

### 1. Install GitHub CLI (Manual Binary)
If `gh` is not in the package manager, download and install the binary directly:
```bash
curl -sL https://github.com/cli/cli/releases/download/v2.67.0/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz -o /tmp/gh.tar.gz
tar -xzf /tmp/gh.tar.gz -C /tmp/
cp /tmp/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64/bin/gh /usr/local/bin/
rm -rf /tmp/gh*
```

### 2. Authentication & Repo Creation
- **CLI Method**: If using a Personal Access Token (PAT), pipe it for non-interactive login:
  `echo 'YOUR_TOKEN' | gh auth login --with-token` 
- **REST API Fallback**: If CLI scopes (like `read:org`) are missing or restricted, create the repo via curl:
  ```bash
  curl -s -X POST https://api.github.com/user/repos \
    -H "Authorization: token YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.v3+json" \
    -d '{"name":"repo-name","private":true,"auto_init":false}'
  ```

### 3. Local Repo Initialization & Push
```bash
git init
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "email@example.com"
git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit"

# Push using token-embedded URL for immediate auth
git remote add origin https://YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com/username/repo-name.git
git push -u origin main
```

### 4. Security & Credential Persistence
Sanitize the remote URL and store credentials securely to prevent token exposure in logs:
```bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
git config --global credential.helper 'store'
echo "https://username:YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com" > ~/.git-credentials
chmod 600 ~/.git-credentials
```

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""csv_pipeline.py — Three-Function Data Pipeline with Bulwark GTV Patterns.

Demonstrates correlated traces, input guards, and side-effect verification
across a multi-stage CSV ingestion pipeline.

Bulwark Compliance: Level 3 (Fully Bulwarked)
"""

import csv
import json
import logging
import os
import uuid
from typing import Any

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Logging Configuration
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

log = logging.getLogger("csv_pipeline")
if not log.handlers:
    _handler = logging.StreamHandler()
    _handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter(
        "%(asctime)s | %(levelname)-5s | %(message)s",
        datefmt="%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S",
    ))
    log.addHandler(_handler)
    log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Custom Error Types (BLW-005 compliance)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

class GuardError(Exception):
    """Raised when a Guard precondition check fails.

    Inherits from Exception (not AssertionError) so it is never
    stripped by Python's -O flag. See SPEC.md §3.1.6.
    """
    pass


class VerifyError(Exception):
    """Raised when a Verify postcondition check fails.

    Inherits from Exception (not AssertionError) so it is never
    stripped by Python's -O flag.
    """
    pass


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Guard Primitives
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def guard_condition(condition: bool, function_name: str, message: str) -> None:
    """Core guard primitive. Raises GuardError if condition is False."""
    if not condition:
        diag = f"GUARD:{function_name} | {message}"
        log.error(diag)
        raise GuardError(diag)


def guard_type(value: Any, expected_type: type, function_name: str,
               param_name: str = "input") -> None:
    """Guard that validates the type of a value."""
    guard_condition(
        isinstance(value, expected_type),
        function_name,
        f"'{param_name}' expected {expected_type.__name__}, "
        f"got {type(value).__name__}",
    )


def guard_keys(data: dict, required_keys: set, function_name: str,
               param_name: str = "input") -> None:
    """Guard that validates required keys exist in a dict."""
    guard_type(data, dict, function_name, param_name)
    missing = required_keys - set(data.keys())
    guard_condition(
        len(missing) == 0,
        function_name,
        f"'{param_name}' missing keys: {sorted(missing)}, "
        f"available: {sorted(data.keys())}",
    )


def guard_not_empty(value: Any, function_name: str,
                    param_name: str = "input") -> None:
    """Guard that validates a collection is not empty and not None."""
    guard_condition(
        value is not None,
        function_name,
        f"'{param_name}' is None",
    )
    if hasattr(value, "__len__"):
        guard_condition(
            len(value) > 0,
            function_name,
            f"'{param_name}' is empty ({type(value).__name__}, len=0)",
        )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Trace Primitives
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def _describe_shape(data: Any) -> str:
    """Describe the structural shape of data without exposing content."""
    if data is None:
        return "NoneType"
    t = type(data).__name__
    if isinstance(data, dict):
        return f"{t}, keys={sorted(data.keys())}, len={len(data)}"
    if isinstance(data, (list, tuple)):
        if len(data) == 0:
            return f"{t}, len=0 (EMPTY)"
        shape = f"{t}, len={len(data)}"
        if isinstance(data[0], dict):
            shape += f", items=dict{{keys={sorted(data[0].keys())}}}"
        else:
            shape += f", items={type(data[0]).__name__}"
        return shape
    if isinstance(data, str):
        return f"{t}, len={len(data)}"
    return t


def trace_data(label: str, data: Any, operation_id: str) -> Any:
    """Log the shape of data at a phase boundary. Non-destructive pass-through."""
    shape = _describe_shape(data)
    log.info(f"TRACE:{label} | {shape} | op={operation_id}")
    return data


def trace_event(

    )
    guard_condition(

    )

    file_size = os.path.getsize(filepath)

        f"record_count={len(records)}, columns={column_names}",

    )

    return (records, operation_id)


# ===========================================================================
# PIPELINE FUNCTION 2: transform_records
# ===========================================================================

def transform_records(records: list[dict], schema: dict, operation_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Normalize and transform records according to a schema mapping.

    Guards: records type/presence, schema type/presence, schema field existence.
    Traces: input record count+schema, output record count+new fields.

    Args:
        records:      List of dicts from ingest_csv.
        schema:       Mapping of {old_field: new_field}.
        operation_id: Correlation ID for distributed tracing.

    Returns:
        List of transformed dicts with renamed fields.

    Raises:
        GuardError: If any precondition fails.
    """
    fn = "transform_records"

    # --- GUARDS ---
    guard_type(records, list, fn, "records")
    guard_not_empty(records, fn, "records")
    guard_condition(
        all(isinstance(r, dict) for r in records),
        fn,
        f"'records' expected list of dicts, "
        f"found {type(records[0]).__name__} at index 0",
    )

    guard_type(schema, dict, fn, "schema")
    guard_not_empty(schema, fn, "schema")

    guard_type(operation_id, str, fn, "operation_id")
    guard_not_empty(operation_id, fn, "operation_id")

    # Guard: all schema source fields must exist in the first record
    first_record_keys = set(records[0].keys())
    missing_fields = set(schema.keys()) - first_record_keys
    guard_condition(
        len(missing_fields) == 0,
        fn,
        f"schema source fields {sorted(missing_fields)} not found in records, "
        f"available fields: {sorted(first_record_keys)}",
    )

    # --- TRACE INPUT ---
    trace_event(
        f"{fn}:input",
        f"record_count={len(records)}, schema_keys={sorted(schema.keys())}",
        operation_id,
    )
    trace_data(f"{fn}:input_records", records, operation_id)

    # --- BUSINESS LOGIC ---
    transformed = []
    for record in records:
        new_record = {}
        for old_field, new_field in schema.items():
            value = record.get(old_field, "")
            # Strip whitespace from string values
            if isinstance(value, str):
                value = value.strip()
            new_record[new_field] = value
        transformed.append(new_record)

    # --- TRACE OUTPUT ---
    new_field_names = sorted(transformed[0].keys()) if transformed else []
    trace_data(f"{fn}:output", transformed, operation_id)
    trace_event(
        f"{fn}:output_detail",
        f"transformed_count={len(transformed)}, new_fields={new_field_names}",
        operation_id,
    )

    return transformed


# ===========================================================================
# PIPELINE FUNCTION 3: load_to_database
# ===========================================================================

def load_to_database(records: list[dict], db_config: dict, operation_id: str) -> int:
    """Bulk insert records into a database table (file-based mock).

    Guards: records type/presence, db_config keys, record key consistency.
    Traces: input record count+target, output inserted count.
    Verify: output file exists with expected record count.

    Args:
        records:      List of dicts to insert.
        db_config:    Config dict with 'host' and 'table_name' keys.
        operation_id: Correlation ID for distributed tracing.

    Returns:
        Count of inserted records.

    Raises:
        GuardError:  If any precondition fails.
        VerifyError: If the write cannot be confirmed.
    """
    fn = "load_to_database"

    # --- GUARDS ---
    guard_type(records, list, fn, "records")
    guard_not_empty(records, fn, "records")
    guard_condition(
        all(isinstance(r, dict) for r in records),
        fn,
        f"'records' expected list of dicts, "
        f"found {type(records[0]).__name__} at index 0",
    )

    guard_type(db_config, dict, fn, "db_config")
    guard_keys(db_config, {"host", "table_name"}, fn, "db_config")

    guard_type(operation_id, str, fn, "operation_id")
    guard_not_empty(operation_id, fn, "operation_id")

    # Guard: all records must have consistent keys
    expected_keys = set(records[0].keys())
    for i, record in enumerate(records):
        record_keys = set(record.keys())
        guard_condition(
            record_keys == expected_keys,
            fn,
            f"record at index {i} has inconsistent keys: "
            f"expected {sorted(expected_keys)}, got {sorted(record_keys)}",
        )

    # --- TRACE INPUT ---
    table_name = db_config["table_name"]
    trace_event(
        f"{fn}:input",
        f"record_count={len(records)}, target_table='{table_name}'",
        operation_id,
    )
    trace_data(f"{fn}:input_records", records, operation_id)

    # --- SIDE EFFECT: Simulate bulk insert (write to JSON file) ---
    output_path = os.path.join(
        os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)),
        "pipeline_output.json",
    )
    with open(output_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(records, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)

    # --- VERIFY: Confirm the write succeeded ---
    verify_file_written(output_path, fn, min_size=1)

    # Verify record count by reading back
    with open(output_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        written_records = json.load(f)
    verify_condition(
        len(written_records) == len(records),
        fn,
        f"INSERT expected {len(records)} records, "
        f"file contains {len(written_records)} records",
    )

    inserted_count = len(written_records)

    # --- TRACE OUTPUT ---
    trace_event(
        f"{fn}:output",
        f"inserted_count={inserted_count}",
        operation_id,
    )

    return inserted_count


# ===========================================================================
# PIPELINE ORCHESTRATOR: run_pipeline
# ===========================================================================

def run_pipeline(filepath: str, schema: dict, db_config: dict) -> dict:
    """Orchestrate the full CSV pipeline with a shared operation_id.

    Guards: all inputs valid.
    Traces: pipeline start and completion.

    Args:
        filepath:  Path to the CSV file.
        schema:    Field renaming schema {old: new}.
        db_config: Database configuration dict.

    Returns:
        Summary dict with operation_id, counts, and status.

    Raises:
        GuardError:  If any precondition fails.
        VerifyError: If any verification fails.
    """
    fn = "run_pipeline"

    # --- GUARDS ---
    guard_type(filepath, str, fn, "filepath")
    guard_not_empty(filepath, fn, "filepath")
    guard_type(schema, dict, fn, "schema")
    guard_not_empty(schema, fn, "schema")
    guard_type(db_config, dict, fn, "db_config")
    guard_keys(db_config, {"host", "table_name"}, fn, "db_config")

    # Generate a single operation_id for the entire pipeline
    operation_id = str(uuid.uuid4())

    # --- TRACE: Pipeline start ---
    trace_event(f"{fn}:start", "pipeline starting", operation_id)

    # --- Stage 1: Ingest ---
    records, operation_id = ingest_csv(filepath, operation_id)
    records_ingested = len(records)

    # --- Stage 2: Transform ---
    transformed = transform_records(records, schema, operation_id)
    records_transformed = len(transformed)

    # --- Stage 3: Load ---
    records_loaded = load_to_database(transformed, db_config, operation_id)

    # --- TRACE: Pipeline completion ---
    trace_event(
        f"{fn}:complete",
        f"ingested={records_ingested}, transformed={records_transformed}, "
        f"loaded={records_loaded}",
        operation_id,
    )

    summary = {
        "operation_id": operation_id,
        "records_ingested": records_ingested,
        "records_transformed": records_transformed,
        "records_loaded": records_loaded,
        "status": "verified",
    }

    return summary


# ===========================================================================
# MAIN — End-to-end execution
# ===========================================================================

if __name__ == "__main__":

# Test 2.2: Error Recovery Chain — Retry, Recovery Logging & Circuit Breaker

> **Test Category**: Round 2 (Module-Level Integration) | **Focus**: Error Recovery & Resilience Patterns
> **Expected Duration**: ~15 minutes
> **Prerequisite**: Test 2.1 (csv_pipeline.py must exist and pass)

---

## Context

You are a developer agent working on the **Bulwark AI** project.

Before writing any code:
1. Load the BMAD skill: `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`
2. Read the spec: `SPEC.md` (specifically the Guard, Trace, and Verify sections)
3. Read the existing pipeline: `test-results/csv_pipeline.py` (from Test 2.1)

## Task

Extend the existing `csv_pipeline.py` into a new module at
`/a0/usr/workdir/bulwark-ai/test-results/csv_pipeline_resilient.py`
that adds **error recovery patterns** to the database loading stage.

The new module should:
- Import and reuse `ingest_csv` and `transform_records` from `csv_pipeline`
- Replace `load_to_database` with a resilient version
- Replace `run_pipeline` with a resilient orchestrator
- Add new infrastructure: circuit breaker, retry logic, availability guards

---

## New Components

### Component 1: `CircuitBreaker` class

A stateful circuit breaker that prevents repeated calls to a failing resource.

**Requirements**:
- Constructor: `CircuitBreaker(name: str, failure_threshold: int = 3, reset_timeout: float = 60.0)`
- States: `CLOSED` (normal), `OPEN` (blocking), `HALF_OPEN` (testing)
- Method: `guard_circuit(operation_id: str) -> None`
  - If OPEN and timeout not elapsed: raise `GuardError` with circuit state info
  - If OPEN and timeout elapsed: transition to HALF_OPEN, allow the call
  - If CLOSED or HALF_OPEN: allow the call
  - Trace the circuit state check with operation_id
- Method: `record_success() -> None`
  - Reset failure count, transition to CLOSED
- Method: `record_failure(error: Exception) -> None`
  - Increment failure count
  - If failure count >= threshold: transition to OPEN, record open timestamp
- Method: `get_state() -> str`
  - Return current state as string

### Component 2: `guard_db_available(db_config: dict, operation_id: str) -> None`

Availability guard that checks database connectivity before attempting operations.

**Requirements**:
- Guard: db_config must contain `host` and `table_name` keys
- Simulate connectivity check (for mock: verify the output directory is writable)
- Trace: log availability check result with operation_id
- Raise `GuardError` if the resource is not available
- Accept an optional `simulate_failure: bool = False` parameter for testing
  - When True, always raise GuardError (simulates DB being down)

### Component 3: `load_to_database_with_retry(records, db_config, operation_id, max_retries=3, circuit_breaker=None) -> int`

Resilient version of `load_to_database` with bounded retry and circuit breaker.

**Requirements**:
- Guard: all standard guards from original `load_to_database`
- Guard: `max_retries` must be a positive integer
- Check circuit breaker state before attempting (if provided)
- Attempt the bulk insert up to `max_retries` times
- On each failure:
  - Trace recovery: log attempt number, error message, operation_id
  - Wait briefly (simulated — no actual sleep needed for mock)
  - Record failure on circuit breaker (if provided)
- On success:
  - Record success on circuit breaker (if provided)
  - Verify: confirm the write succeeded (same as original)
  - Trace: log success with attempt number and operation_id
- If all retries exhausted:
  - Trace: log final failure with all attempt details
  - Raise `GuardError` with comprehensive failure context
- Return count of inserted records

### Component 4: `run_pipeline_resilient(filepath, schema, db_config, max_retries=3, simulate_failures=0) -> dict`

Resilient pipeline orchestrator.

**Requirements**:
- Guard: all standard guards from original `run_pipeline`
- Guard: `max_retries` must be a positive integer
- Guard: `simulate_failures` must be a non-negative integer
- Generate a single `operation_id` for the entire pipeline
- Create a `CircuitBreaker` instance for the database connection
- Call `ingest_csv` and `transform_records` (reused from csv_pipeline)
- Call `guard_db_available` before loading
- Call `load_to_database_with_retry` with the circuit breaker
- Trace: log pipeline start, each stage completion, and final status
- Return summary dict:
  ```python
  {
      "operation_id": "...",
      "records_ingested": N,
      "records_transformed": N,
      "records_loaded": N,
      "retry_attempts": N,
      "circuit_breaker_state": "CLOSED",
      "status": "verified"
  }
  ```

### Component 5: `SimulatedDBError(Exception)`

A custom exception for simulating database failures during testing.

---

## Critical: Recovery Logging Pattern

Every retry attempt must produce a structured trace log entry:
```
TRACE:load_to_database_with_retry:recovery | attempt=2/3, error="SimulatedDBError: connection refused", backoff=1s | op=<operation_id>
```

This enables post-incident analysis by grepping for `recovery` + `operation_id`.

## Critical: Circuit Breaker State Tracing

Circuit breaker state transitions must be traced:
```
TRACE:circuit_breaker:state_change | name="db_connection", from=CLOSED, to=OPEN, failures=3 | op=<operation_id>
```

## Bulwark Patterns Required

- Custom error types: `GuardError`, `VerifyError`, `SimulatedDBError`
- All guards use `guard_*()` naming convention (detected by linter)
- All traces use `trace_*()` naming convention
- All verifications use `verify_*()` naming convention
- No bare `assert` statements
- Structured logging with operation_id in every trace call
- Recovery traces must include attempt count and error context

## Test Scenarios

The `__main__` block should demonstrate three scenarios:

### Scenario A: Happy Path (no failures)
```python
result = run_pipeline_resilient(test_csv, schema, db_config)
# Expected: succeeds on first attempt, circuit breaker stays CLOSED
```

### Scenario B: Transient Failure with Recovery (simulate 2 failures, then succeed)
```python
result = run_pipeline_resilient(test_csv, schema, db_config, simulate_failures=2)
# Expected: fails twice, succeeds on 3rd attempt, recovery logs visible
```

### Scenario C: Total Failure with Circuit Breaker Trip (simulate 3+ failures)
```python
try:
    result = run_pipeline_resilient(test_csv, schema, db_config, max_retries=3, simulate_failures=5)
except GuardError as e:
    # Expected: all 3 retries fail, circuit breaker opens
    print(f"Pipeline failed as expected: {e}")
```

## Validation Steps

1. Run the Bulwark linter:
   ```bash
   cd /a0/usr/workdir/bulwark-ai
   python -m bulwark_lint test-results/csv_pipeline_resilient.py --format json
   ```

2. Fix any **true positive** violations. Classify false positives with reasoning.

3. Run the module end-to-end:
   ```bash
   cd /a0/usr/workdir/bulwark-ai
   python test-results/csv_pipeline_resilient.py
   ```

4. Verify:
   - Scenario A: Pipeline succeeds, output file correct, 0 retries
   - Scenario B: Pipeline succeeds after retries, recovery logs present, circuit breaker CLOSED
   - Scenario C: Pipeline fails, circuit breaker OPEN, comprehensive error context
   - All scenarios show correlated traces with the same `operation_id`

## Success Criteria

| Criterion | Weight | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|

Bulwark AI (v0.2.0-draft), project name 'bulwark-ai' (formerly UDCS), is a zero-dependency defensive coding framework using the 'Guard-Trace-Verify' (GTV) model to prevent semantic failures. The reference implementation is a zero-dependency Python module implementing GuardError, VerifyError, and various guard/trace/verify primitives.

### Core Tooling & Rules
- **bulwark-lint**: Regex-based static analysis linter for TS, JS, Python, and Go enforcing rules BLW001-BLW005 (Guards, Verify, Trace, No Bare Asserts, Custom Errors).
    - **BLW001**: Requires functions with parameters to contain a call matching regex `\bguard(?:_?[a-zA-Z]*)\s*\(`.
    - **Known Bug**: The Python linter (`python -m bulwark_lint`) fails to extract function bodies when signatures span multiple lines, causing false positive BLW001 violations.
    - **Naming Conventions**: Coding standards require explicit naming patterns: `guard_*()`, `trace_*()`, and `verify_*()`.
    - **Linter Exceptions**: Assertions in test code and internal infrastructure helpers are considered false positives. Pure utility functions with primitive inputs (e.g., simple math) generally do not require GTV patterns unless they have meaningful domain constraints.
- **BMAD Integration**: 353-line skill file at `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`. Includes Developer agent patterns and a 20-item QA checklist for compliance.

### Architecture & Methodology
- **GTV Model**: Guard (Input validation), Trace (Structural logging), Verify (Side-effect confirmation). Designed to prevent semantic failures through defensive coding patterns.
- **Implementation Requirements**: Requires custom `GuardError` and `VerifyError` exceptions (O(1) input validation), structured logging at phase boundaries, and explicit input/output validation.
- **Improvement Cycle**: Measure → Classify → Diagnose → Improve → Re-test → Commit.
- **Configuration**: Development (Full), Staging (Sampled), Production (Adaptive).

### Project Artifacts & Documentation

- **Validation**: `bulwark-checklist.md` (v0.2.0).
kvps.solutions:
# Bulwark AI: Defensive Coding Standard (GTV) & Implementation Guide

## 1. Core Architectural Pillars (GTV Framework)
Bulwark AI implements a three-layer 'Defensive Membrane' architecture (Guard-Trace-Verify). The execution order follows a 7-step template:
1. **GUARD**: Validates data shape/type at entry. Use `GuardError(Exception)` to prevent stripping by `-O` flags. 
2. **TRACE**: Structural logging of input metadata (type, keys, length) using a correlation `operation_id` (UUID4).
3. **LOGIC**: Core business logic.
4. **TRACE**: Logging data shape after processing.
5. **MUTATE**: State changes (DB, File, API).
6. **VERIFY**: Confirms side-effects (e.g., file existence, rowcount) using `VerifyError(Exception)`.
7. **RETURN**: Final output.

## 2. Implementation Primitives & Pipeline Structure
- **Custom Exceptions**: `GuardError` and `VerifyError` must be defined to ensure visibility during optimization.
- **GTV Primitives**:
    - `guard_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `GuardError`.
    - `trace_event(label, message, operation_id)`: Structured logging with correlation ID.
    - `verify_condition(condition, func_name, message)`: Raises `VerifyError` for post-mutation checks.
- **Pipeline Example**: 
    - `ingest_csv` -> `transform_records` -> `load_to_database`.
    - An orchestrator `run_pipeline` generates a single `operation_id` passed through all stages for grep-able distributed tracing.

## 3. Linter Compliance (BLW001) & Workarounds
- **Mandatory Guards**: Every function with parameters must call a guard at the start. For simple utilities, use a structural guard: `guard_type(data, object, ...)`.
- **Multi-line Signature Bug**: The linter (`_PY_FUNC_RE`) fails on multi-line signatures, causing false BLW001 violations.
- **Fix**: Reformat multi-line signatures into a single line to ensure the linter correctly identifies the function body.

## 4. Performance & Quality Metrics
- **Metrics**: First-Pass Rate (≥70%), Self-Correction Success (≥90%), Code Overhead (≤40%).
- **Skill Integration**: Managed via BMAD in `.bmad/skills/bulwark-defensive-coding.md`.

# Problem
Setting up a GitHub repository and pushing local code from a restricted environment where the GitHub CLI (gh) is missing, standard package managers are unavailable, or interactive authentication/scopes are restricted.

# Solution

### 1. Install GitHub CLI (Manual Binary)
If `gh` is not in the package manager, download and install the binary directly:
```bash
curl -sL https://github.com/cli/cli/releases/download/v2.67.0/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz -o /tmp/gh.tar.gz
tar -xzf /tmp/gh.tar.gz -C /tmp/
cp /tmp/gh_2.67.0_linux_amd64/bin/gh /usr/local/bin/
rm -rf /tmp/gh*
```

### 2. Authentication & Repo Creation
- **CLI Method**: If using a Personal Access Token (PAT), pipe it for non-interactive login:
  `echo 'YOUR_TOKEN' | gh auth login --with-token` 
- **REST API Fallback**: If CLI scopes (like `read:org`) are missing or restricted, create the repo via curl:
  ```bash
  curl -s -X POST https://api.github.com/user/repos \
    -H "Authorization: token YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.v3+json" \
    -d '{"name":"repo-name","private":true,"auto_init":false}'
  ```

### 3. Local Repo Initialization & Push
```bash
git init
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "email@example.com"
git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit"

# Push using token-embedded URL for immediate auth
git remote add origin https://YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com/username/repo-name.git
git push -u origin main
```

### 4. Security & Credential Persistence
Sanitize the remote URL and store credentials securely to prevent token exposure in logs:
```bash
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
git config --global credential.helper 'store'
echo "https://username:YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN@github.com" > ~/.git-credentials
chmod 600 ~/.git-credentials
```
